title: Capital Allocation
tagline: How money shapes what gets built
description: The mechanisms through which capital flows determine what exists in the world — from venture backing of startups to public market investment to debt structures to government spending. Encompasses the psychology of investing (hype cycles, groupthink, bubbles), frameworks for calculating ROI across different asset classes, and the comparative effectiveness of different capital deployment mechanisms.

feed.author: Meghan Reynolds
feed.content: Heard from LPs this week: The past 9 months have felt like groundhog's day - a very small set of deals dominating all LP convos. The fever pitch to access rounds of OpenAI and Anthropic by LPs (and even GPs calling us) at times - has reached levels I've never seen...

feed.author: Kevin
feed.content: This man achieved 921% returns while the market made 117%. He retired at 45 after making investors $2 billion. Yet you've probably never heard of him because he refused every interview and turned away new money. Here's Nick Sleep's secret to finding 100-baggers.

feed.author: Erik Torenberg
feed.content: Book notes from Thomas Piketty's "Capital in the 21st Century" — Curious to hear critiques & responses to the book.

feed.author: Ram Bhupatiraju
feed.content: ROIC is one of the most important concepts in Investing. Excellent article with a deep dive on that topic with couple of examples, and also some terrific links at the end.

title: Technological Innovation
description: The study of how technology advances, what makes a breakthrough (vs. incremental improvement), and how markets, social behavior, and institutional structures shape the pace and direction of innovation. Includes analysis of specific technologies and sectors, identification of emerging trends, and understanding the interplay between invention (technical possibility) and adoption (market/social reality).

feed.author: Meer | AI Tools & News
feed.content: This guy literally scraped 25,000 comments to uncover which AI tools are actually making people money (and saving hours).

feed.author: Bonnie Kavoussi
feed.content: Peter Thiel argues that science was "decentralized" and "healthy" before the New Deal; then after the atomic bomb, it became too centralized and bureaucratized to innovate. Before, we had "science as discovery," now we have "science as governance."

title: Company Building
description: How companies are built as enduring organizations — including organizational structure, employee incentives, cultural norms, and operational systems. Extends beyond conventional startups to innovation institutions (Bell Labs, Disney's Imagineers, IBM's Wild Ducks, Edison's Muckers) and alternative ownership/incentive models. The question is always: what makes an organization capable of sustained excellence?

feed.author: Joel Gascoigne
feed.content: I've been focused on getting Buffer through this period as unscathed as possible. One debt I believe is building in companies right now is burnout. To lessen that impact at Buffer, we'll try a 4-day workweek (at full pay) for May.

feed.author: Brianne Kimmel
feed.content: One of the most challenging exercises for early stage companies is defining your initial GTM strategy. Shares his playbook as the first Head of Sales & Customer Success at Typeform and Notion.

title: Religious Intellectualism
tagline: Faith, reason, and the search for ultimate meaning
description: The pursuit of religious truth through rigorous intellectual inquiry, including Mormon apologetics and philosophy, Christian apologetics more broadly, and the reconciliation of faith and science. The core interest is treating religious questions with the same intellectual seriousness as any other domain — not faith vs. reason, but faith through reason.

title: City Building
tagline: The design of human flourishing at scale
description: What makes cities work as engines of human potential — quality of life, effective services, vibrant culture, economic opportunity, all delivered through competent governance. Includes the study of visionary city projects (Walt Disney's EPCOT, Joseph Smith's Nauvoo, modern New Cities like California Forever), and the question of whether cities can be built intentionally around companies.

projects.title: Prosperable
projects.description: Building a universal benchmark to make every community the best place to live.

title: People Over Politics
tagline: Policy through the lens of human outcomes
description: An attempt to understand policy divorced from partisan tribalism — focused instead on what actually works to solve problems like crime, homelessness, inflation, unemployment, immigration, and energy. The belief that good solutions exist and can be identified through honest evaluation of evidence and human impact.

title: Historical Futurism
tagline: Learning from how the past imagined tomorrow
description: The study of history not as a static record but as a laboratory of attempted futures — how did people in the past envision progress, what solutions did they try, what worked, what failed, and what remains relevant? Encompasses classic histories, the history of ideas through science fiction, and the mining of old proposals for insights applicable now.

feed.author: David Perell
feed.content: If you're looking for new ideas about the future, I recommend The Sovereign Individual. I've never seen a book with so many correct predictions, and it's inspired a generation of builders, entrepreneurs, and technologists.

title: The Language of Discourse
tagline: Rhetoric, persuasion, and the mechanics of shared understanding
description: How language shapes collective action — the study of rhetoric (Cicero, John Quincy Adams), the mechanics of persuasion (manifestos, vision statements, rallying cries), and the darker arts of manipulation and propaganda. The goal is to understand both how to communicate truth effectively and how to recognize when language is being weaponized against truth.

title: Ideological Family Tree
tagline: Preserving and transmitting wisdom across generations
description: The commitment to creating a multi-generational record of ideas, stories, and wisdom through systematic documentation — weekly letters to children, personal journaling, interviews with family members, and the curation of a family intellectual history. The belief that ideas and values compound across generations only if they're deliberately preserved and transmitted.

title: The Republic of Letters 2.0
tagline: The new infrastructure of distributed intellectual production
description: The transformation of knowledge creation from institutionally-gatekept to distributed networks of independent thinkers. Historically, the Republic of Letters bypassed official institutions through correspondence networks that catalyzed the Enlightenment. Today, the internet enables a similar dynamic at scale. The questions are: What are the new social technologies for intellectual exchange? How do credibility and quality emerge without formal gatekeeping?

feed.author: Lolita Taub
feed.content: Founders: @ycombinator just launched the YC Startup Library for you! It includes +15 years worth of: videos, podcasts, essays.

title: Open-Source Knowledge
description: The belief that knowledge should be treated like open-source software — living, interconnected, forkable, and in continuous development — rather than static publications that slowly decay. This encompasses the study and construction of knowledge management systems, collaborative knowledge platforms, and the philosophy that ideas compound fastest when they're publicly legible, modular, and built to be extended by others.

feed.author: George Mack
feed.content: Josh Waitzkin might be the most INTERESTING person alive. He doesn't have Twitter. And he barely uses the internet. I've compiled my favorite 5 MENTAL MODELS of his below.

feed.author: David Perell
feed.content: "There should be a good online class that anyone can take on ANY topic, and right now, there isn't. That, to me, is just criminal." — Tyler Cowen
